result:
I’ve been thinking a lot about silence lately. Not the absence of sound, but the kind of silence that exists beneath the noise—the quiet hum of a refrigerator in an empty kitchen at 3 AM, the space between breaths when you’re waiting for news, the soft, woolly quiet of falling snow that seems to absorb the very concept of echo. We live in a world that has declared war on this silence. Our pockets buzz with curated outrage, our homes chatter with virtual assistants, our commutes are padded with podcasts preaching self-optimization. We have become connoisseurs of the fill, terrified of the gap. But what if the gap is where we actually live?

I remember, as a child, long car journeys before the era of in-seat screens. I’d stare out the window at the hypnotic blur of fence posts and telephone wires, my mind untethered, drifting into stories of my own making. There was nothing to “consume.” The boredom was fertile, rich, and deep. From that blankness sprang elaborate fantasies, observations about the changing light on fields, and a profound, uncomplicated sense of being. That boredom, that silence, wasn’t an emptiness to be filled. It was a fullness to be experienced. We have systematically engineered that state out of existence for our children and for ourselves, and I wonder if we are poorer for it.

Because creativity isn’t born from a cascade of inputs; it’s born from the digestion of experience. It needs the blank page, the idle walk, the moment of staring at the ceiling. The muse, as they say, doesn’t visit on a schedule, but she certainly never finds you in the middle of a TikTok scroll. She finds you in the shower, or while you’re washing dishes, when your conscious mind is occupied with a simple task and your subconscious is free to play, to connect dots you didn’t even know were on the same page. We’ve barricaded the doors to our own subconscious with a relentless feed of other people’s thoughts, other people’s aesthetics, other people’s dramas.

This isn’t a Luddite rant against technology. The connectivity, the knowledge, the art—it’s miraculous. It’s the imbalance that’s the poison. We’ve forgotten how to be alone with ourselves. The very phrase “alone with myself” has taken on a faintly ominous, clinical tone, as if it’s a state to be diagnosed and treated. Solitude is conflated with loneliness, and silence with awkwardness. We patch over every potential pause in conversation, we soundtrack every activity, we treat our own internal company as somehow insufficient.

I’ve started to practice small rebellions. I’ll drive somewhere without turning on the radio or a podcast. The first ten minutes are itchy. My mind, like a caged animal, rattles its bars: *Check the news. Plan the week. Worry about that email.* But if I persist, something shifts. The mental chatter begins to settle, like snowflakes landing on a disturbed pond. I start to *see* the drive—the particular slant of a roof, the way a certain tree holds its leaves, the architecture of clouds. I arrive not frazzled and mentally cluttered from digesting someone else’s narrative, but strangely settled, present. I’ve re-inhabited my own senses.

The same happens on walks. Leaving the phone behind—not just in the pocket, but physically absent—is a radical act. Without the option to document, to share, to look something up, the walk becomes purely experiential. You are not walking *for* steps or photos or content. You are walking to walk. You notice the gritty texture of the path underfoot, the complex symphony of bird calls layered not as “background noise” but as a specific conversation, the smell of damp earth and coming rain. You become a participant in the world, not a commentator on it. Your mind, freed from its role as perpetual curator and broadcaster, stretches out. It meanders. It solves problems you weren’t consciously working on. It remembers forgotten things. It simply *is*.

This cultivated silence is also the bedrock of listening—real listening. Not the pause-where-you-formulate-your-next-response kind, but the deep, receptive listening where you truly take in another person. You hear not just their words, but the tremor beneath them, the spaces they leave, the meaning that lives in the cadence of their voice. That kind of listening requires an inner quiet, a stilling of your own internal radio station. It is an act of profound generosity and connection that our rapid-fire, opinion-laden discourse desperately lacks.

Perhaps what we fear in silence is ourselves. The un-curated, un-branded, un-optimized self. The thoughts that aren’t witty enough for Twitter, the feelings that are too messy for Instagram, the slow, mundane, un-shareable realities of being a human animal on a planet that spins through a silent, star-dusted vacuum. In the silence, we meet our own fragility, our unanswered questions, our sheer, unadorned existence. It can be uncomfortable. It is also the only place where authenticity has room to grow.

So, I am building a room inside myself. A room with no Wi-Fi, no refresh button, no metrics. The walls are plain, the light is soft. It is here I go to not *do*, but to *be*. To let the dust of the day settle. To hear the faint, old music of my own heartbeat, the rhythm that was there before any algorithm learned my name. In this quiet, I am not a consumer, a content creator, a data point, or a brand. I am a collection of memories and senses, a work in perpetual, slow progress. The world’s noise is a river rushing by outside, powerful and compelling. But this silence is the deep, still well. And from its dark, undisturbed water, I can finally hear my own truth, drop by precious drop, echoing back up at me. It is the sound of coming home.
